coredata.dc:title: A triple-emission ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on carbon dots-Au nanoclusters nanocomposite for detection of tetracycline
coredata.dc:creator: Miao, Jiaqi
Ji, Wan
Yu, Jialuo
Cheng, Jianxia
Huang, Yan
Arabi, Maryam
Zhou, Na
Li, Bowei
Zhang, Zhiyang
Chen, Lingxin
Wang, Xiaoyan
coredata.dc:description: The accumulation of excess tetracycline (TC) antibiotics in the environment are seriously dangerous to ecosystems and human health. Herein, a well-defined triple-emission ratiometric fluorescence sensor was developed for the detection of TC by using the carbon dots hybridized glycoprotein ovalbumin stabilized Au nanoclusters nanocomposite (CDs-AuNCs). The construction of this triple-emission sensing method was simple and didn’t require assistance of complex coupling reagents. Upon TC recognition, the intrinsic green fluorescence at 520 nm of TC was significantly intensified by OVA sensitization, whereas the blue and red fluorescence (at 423 and 660 nm) of CDs-AuNCs sensor were gradually quenched through the inner filter effect and dynamic quenching process. Under optimized conditions, the CDs-AuNCs sensor exhibited a wide linear response for TC in the range of 0.5–40 μM with a detection limit down to 0.056 μM, and the corresponding fluorescence colors changed from blue purple to pink, orange red, yellow, and finally yellow green under a UV lamp, which can be easily discerned by naked eye. Moreover, the sensor showed excellent selectivity for TC, and was successfully applied in real water samples. The simple, rapid, reliable and visible CDs-AuNCs sensor proved to be potentially applicable for TC determination in complicated matrices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Tetracycline antibiotic
Ratiometric fluorescence sensor
Triple-emission
CDs-AuNCs nanocomposite
Visual detection

Effects of experimental conditions on the detection
Analytical performance of the ratiometric fluorescence sensing

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of CDs-AuNCs (insert: HRTEM image of CDs-AuNCs), (B) FT-IR spectra of CDs (black line), AuNCs (red line) and CDs-AuNCs (blue line).

(A) UV−vis absorption spectra of the CDs (blue line), AuNCs (red line) and CDs-AuNCs (purple line). (B) Fluorescence emission (yellow line) and excitation (purple line) spectra of CDs-AuNCs. Effect of (C) temperature and (E) pH on the fluorescence intensity of CDs-AuNCs, (D) fluorescence intensities of CDs-AuNCs under the excitation of 360 nm beam for different periods of time.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs-AuNCs before and after the addition of TC. (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of OVA, TC and OVA+TC mixture with excitation at 360 nm. (C) The UV–vis spectrum of TC and fluorescence excitation spectrum of CDs-AuNCs. The fluorescence lifetime of fluorescence emission peak at 423 nm (D) and 660 nm (E) of CDs-AuNCs in the absence and presence of TC at a concentration of 25 μM.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of CDs-AuNCs with addition of the different concentrations of TC (0.5–40 μM) and (B) linear relationship between (I 520/I 423/I 660)/(I 520/I 423/I 660)0 and the concentration of TC, λex= 360 nm. The inset was the photography image of CDs-AuNCs with different concentrations of TC under 365 nm UV lamp.

(I 520/I 423/I 660)/(I 520/I 423/I 660)0 variation of CDs-AuNCs on the addition of various analogues or TC and corresponding images under 365 nm UV lamp. (Concentrations of TC and other analogues were 10 μM). Oxytetracycline (OTC), chlortetracycline (CTC), serine (SER), threonine (THR), glutathione (GSH), clindamycin (CLDM), ciprofloxacin (CIP), enrofloxacin (ENR), sulfanilamide (SA) and sulfamerazine (SMR) were chosen as the analogues.
Schematic illustration of CDs-AuNCs sensor synthesis and the fluorescence assays of TC.
Spiked recoveries and RSDs (%, n = 3) for the determination of TC in real samples by using the proposed fluorescent sensor and HPLC method.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Average value from three individual experiments.

head.ce:title: A triple-emission ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on carbon dots-Au nanoclusters nanocomposite for detection of tetracycline

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CAS Key Laboratory of Coastal Environmental Processes and Ecological Remediation, Shandong Key Laboratory of Coastal Environmental Processes, Research Centre for Coastal Environmental Engineering and Technology, Yantai Institute of Coastal Zone Research, Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The accumulation of excess tetracycline (TC) antibiotics in the environment are seriously dangerous to ecosystems and human health. Herein, a well-defined triple-emission ratiometric fluorescence sensor was developed for the detection of TC by using the carbon dots hybridized glycoprotein ovalbumin stabilized Au nanoclusters nanocomposite (CDs-AuNCs). The construction of this triple-emission sensing method was simple and didn’t require assistance of complex coupling reagents. Upon TC recognition, the intrinsic green fluorescence at 520 nm of TC was significantly intensified by OVA sensitization, whereas the blue and red fluorescence (at 423 and 660 nm) of CDs-AuNCs sensor were gradually quenched through the inner filter effect and dynamic quenching process. Under optimized conditions, the CDs-AuNCs sensor exhibited a wide linear response for TC in the range of 0.5–40 μM with a detection limit down to 0.056 μM, and the corresponding fluorescence colors changed from blue purple to pink, orange red, yellow, and finally yellow green under a UV lamp, which can be easily discerned by naked eye. Moreover, the sensor showed excellent selectivity for TC, and was successfully applied in real water samples. The simple, rapid, reliable and visible CDs-AuNCs sensor proved to be potentially applicable for TC determination in complicated matrices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the continuous production and overuse of antibiotics, the pollution of antibiotics in the environment is becoming more and more serious. Among them, tetracycline (TC), as one of the commonly used antibiotics, it is mainly used in the prevention and treatment of human and animal diseases, as well as in aquaculture, livestock and poultry breeding, etc [1]. However, the continuous accumulation of TC content in water will destroy the balance of the ecosystem, and be transmitted in the food chain through biological enrichment, thus causing harm to the entire ecological environment and human health [2]. The traditional methods such as high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [3], liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) [4], enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay [5] for TC sensing usually have highly accuracy and sensitivity, but still suffer from several impediments, like expensive instruments, complicated operation process, professional technicians [6]. Therefore, it is essential to develop stable, efficient, and specific analytical methods for determination of TC.

More recently, fluorescence-based sensor has been applied to TC detection because of its fast response, simple operation, high sensitivity and easy visual analysis [7]. Compared to single-emission mode, ratiometric fluorescence sensors record two or more independent fluorescence signals in interaction with the analyte, which can effectively improve the signal-to-noise ratio and enable reliable quantitative analysis of analytes [8]. Nowadays, the ratiometric fluorescence sensors for TC analysis almost provide dual emission [9,10], and their color composition modes mainly involve blue-red, green-red, and green-blue in analyte recognition. However, the color changing windows are still not wide enough. It is considered that a new fluorescence emission peak can be introduced to construct the multiple-emission sensors [11,12], which can not only ensure the widest visual color range, but also effectively improve the TC detection sensitivity.

It has been reported that the fluorescence emission of TC itself is very weak. However, TC can interact with protein and enter the hydrophobic binding cavity of protein, while its rotation is limited, resulting in the fluorescence sensitization effect of protein on TC [13]. On the other hand, based on the protein acted as both reducing agent and stabilizing agent, the promising gold nanoclusters (Au NCs) can be a strong candidate for current fluorescence material selection. This synthesis route is simple, economical and mild, which is an ideal green synthesis strategy [14]. OVA is a typical glycoprotein originated from chicken egg white, and the red-emission OVA stabilized AuNCs have widely attracted attention due to its strong emission and excellent biocompatibility. In addition, carbon dots (CDs) have been extensively employed as a new alternative of organic fluorophores to construct the fluorescent sensor, which possess several advantages such as facile synthesis route, excellent fluorescence properties, cost-effective, and low cytotoxicity [15,16].

Most of the current dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence sensors based on AuNCs and CDs are synthesized separately and then chemically coupled or simply doped, which is cumbersome and prone to impurity interference [17,18]. Inspired by the boronate affinity between boronic acid and the cis-diol-containing structures of glycoproteins, we can choose boron-doped CDs and OVA stabilized AuNCs to develop the nanocomposite as ratiometric fluorescence sensor. Importantly, the blue-emissive CDs, red-emissive AuNCs and the green-emissive of TC sensitization can be clearly distinguished, and their quenching or enhancing degree is significantly different.

In this work, we proposed a facile approach to synthesize triple-emission ratiometric fluorescence sensor for naked-eye detection of TC. 3-aminophenylboronic acid (APBA) was used as the carbon source to fabricate the boron-doped CDs through one-pot hydrothermal method without further passivation. Then, based on the sufficient interaction between OVA and CDs, the CDs hybridized OVA stabilized AuNCs nanocomposite, namely CDs-AuNCs was quickly and easily prepared. With the increasing concentration of TC, the red and blue-emission of CDs-AuNCs were quenched at different rates, whereas the green fluorescence of TC was gradually enhanced by the sensitization effect of OVA on TC. Furthermore, as the triple-emission intensity ratios vary, the fluorescence color variation of CDs-AuNCs can vary over the blue-red-green window for naked eye discernment. Accordingly, the novel constructed sensor displayed sensitivity and selectivity to TC and provides a convenient sensing strategy for visual sensing of antibiotics in real water samples.
The details of material and methods are presented in Supporting Information.
Materials and instruments; Synthesis of carbon dots (CDs); Synthesis of CDs-AuNCs; Triple-emission ratiometric fluorescent detection of TC and Analysis of real sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of CDs-AuNCs sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The criterion for the selection of 3-aminophenylboronic acid (3-APBA) as the carbon source is the abundant boron-containing functional groups in its structure, which remains after hydrothermal carbonization. Then, the addition of OVA was preferentially combined with CDs via the specific binding between the cis-diol bond of glycoprotein and the borate groups on the surface of CDs. Finally, the CDs-functionalized OVA were involved in the synthesis of AuNCs as the reducing agent and stabilizing agent and the CDs-AuNCs nanocomposite was obtained ( Scheme 1). Different from the traditional dual-emission fluorescence methods such as coupling fluorescent materials with coupling agents, this work is simpler and more environmentally friendly, and does not require the assistance of complex reagents.

From the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image ( Fig. 1A), CDs-AuNCs had a nearly spherical morphology and exhibited good dispersion with an average diameter of approximately 3–4 nm. As measured from the HRTEM image, the lattice spacing of the constituent nanocomposite was 0.217 nm, which is very close to the hexagonal pattern of graphene with d1100 [19]. Moreover, as CDs-AuNCs nanocomposite were obtained from the CDs hybridized OVA stabilized AuNCs, it can be observed in the TEM of Fig. S1 that the particle size is obviously larger than that of CDs and AuNCs alone. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was used to characterize the functional groups. From Fig. 1B, the peak at 3376 cm−1 in the FT-IR curve of CDs corresponds to the stretching vibration of O-H. The characteristic peaks at 1581, 1618 and 1490 cm−1 are vibrational peaks for CC, CO and N–H, respectively [20]. In addition, the vibrations correspond to the asymmetric B−O stretching mode are observed at 1371 and 1430 cm−1, while the absorption peaks of 1030 and 1167 cm−1 are attributed to the C-B and B-O-H stretching vibration, respectively [21,22]. Thus, it can be confirmed that there are abundant boric acid groups on the CDs surface, which provides conditions for the subsequent synthesis of CDs-AuNCs nanocomposite. The FT-IR spectrum analysis of AuNCs (Fig. 1B, red curve) displayed a clear broad transmission band ∼3398 cm−1, corresponding to O-H groups is observed. Two clear characteristic peaks at 1541 and 1644 cm−1 are corresponded to the amide groups which is consistent with the structure of the OVA protein [23]. As expected, CDs-AuNCs nanocomposite possess both CDs as well as the characteristic peak of AuNCs. In addition, the disappearance of the characteristic peak at 1167 cm−1 (B-O-H) is clearly observed in the FT-IR spectrum of CDs-AuNCs, indicating that the hybridization of the nanocomposite is indeed assembled through the interaction of the boronic acid group with the cis-diol bond of cis-dihydroxy group on OVA.

The optical properties of CDs-AuNCs were further investigated using UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopies. As shown in Fig. 2A, the UV–vis spectra of CDs exhibit the absorption peaks at 290 nm corresponds to the π-π * transition of the CC. The absorbance in the range 317–420 nm was ascribable to the n-π * transition for the CO groups [24]. As for CDs-AuNCs, the absorption peak is around 275 nm, which was characteristic for AuNCs, indicated that the AuNCs was further hybrid with CDs. The fluorescence property of CDs-AuNCs was also investigated as shown in Fig. 2B. The excitation spectrum indicated that the excitation peak of CDs-AuNCs is located near 360 nm, so 360 nm is used as the excitation wavelength for subsequent experimental studies. The emission wavelength of CDs-AuNCs exhibits two emission peaks centered at 423 nm and 660 nm, respectively, of which 423 nm is derived from CDs obtained with 3-APBA as the precursor material, and the emission peak at 660 nm is the AuNCs prepared by reducing HAuCl4 with OVA, resulting in a red emission peak. Additionally, the CDs-AuNCs display a highly resistant to temperature change range of 20 − 60 °C (Fig. 2C). As shown in Fig. 2D, CDs-AuNCs exhibit high photostability, since their fluorescence intensity almost keep constant under continuous 60 min irradiation. And CDs-AuNCs show stable fluorescence intensity at pH 8.5–10.0 (Fig. 2E), which exactly matched the pH with the test of TC.

As described in Fig. 3A, the fluorescence intensity at 423 nm and 660 nm of CDs-AuNCs sensor was quenched by TC addition, while a new increase fluorescence emission peak at 520 nm. TC had a weak fluorescence emission on its own, but the fluorescence intensity can be obviously enhanced after interacting with OVA under 360 nm excitation (Fig. 3B). Therefore, we speculate that it is the sensitization of the protein OVA in the CDs-AuNCs sensor with TC that achieves the significant enhancement of the newly generated fluorescence peak. The OVA protein molecule has the three-dimensional hydrophobic cavities [25], which can be able to act as sensitizers and combine with TC through intermolecular interactions [14]. The entry of TC molecules into the hydrophobic cavities of OVA leads to the aggregation of TC molecules and the restriction of their rotational motion, resulting in the obvious fluorescence enhancement.

As for the quenched blue fluorescence peak at 423 nm of CDs-AuNCs sensor after the addition of TC, the quenching mechanism was further explored. Since TC had the distinct absorption band at 250–400 nm, which coincidentally overlapped with the excitation wavelength of CDs-AuNCs (Fig. 3C), the quenching mechanism was assumed to be the inner filter effect (IFE) or Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) [26]. Moreover, as presented in Fig. 3D, the average fluorescence lifetime for emission peak at 423 nm of CDs-AuNCs sensor were performed in the absence and presence of TCs, which respectively were 2.86 and 2.60 ns. The approximately unchanged lifetime revealed that the blue fluorescence quenching of CDs-AuNCs sensor by TC is probably the IFE sensing process. Furthermore, we also investigate the fluorescence decay of red fluorescence peaks at 660 nm as shown in Fig. 3E. The average fluorescence lifetimes of CDs-AuNCs with TC (0.91 μs) was shorter than that of CDs-AuNCs sensor (1.37 μs), implying that the actual quenching mechanism for red fluorescence peak at 660 nm is dominated by dynamic quenching, and static type can be excluded [27]. When TC was added, the Au-S bond of AuNCs was broken by the competitive interactions between TC and OVA, which can lead to the destruction of AuNCs stabilized by OVA [14]. Consequently, the prepared triple-emission fluorescence sensor was successfully constructed, and can effectively ratiometric recognize TC based on the comprehensive detection mechanism of protein sensitization, the IFE and dynamic quenching process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of experimental conditions on the detection
ce:section.ce:para: Various parameters affecting the sensing performance of CDs-AuNCs sensor were investigated, mainly involving the synthesis conditions of CDs-AuNCs, and the detection conditions for TC.

As displayed in Fig. S2, the more the dosage of OVA, the more significant the sensitization effect on TC will be. However, for comparing the fluorescence color changes under UV light, CDs-AuNCs with 20 mg OVA dose will have obvious fluorescence color in blue purple-orange-yellow-yellow green as TC addition, while the overall color change at 15 mg OVA was yellowish, and the color change at 25 mg OVA was bluish green. Thus 20 mg was selected for the follow-up study of the sensor. In addition, we also investigated the dosage of different HAuCl4, as shown in Fig. S3. It is obvious that the intensity of the emission peak at 660 nm enhanced with the increase of the amount of HAuCl4. When the amount of HAuCl4 is too small (Fig. S3 A and B), the color of the whole system will change to bluish, while too much HAuCl4 will cause the overall reddish phenomenon. Therefore, also considering the color change, 355 μL HAuCl4 was selected as the dosage for subsequent research.

For more sensitive detection of TC, the triple-emission intensity ratios of CDs-AuNCs sensor were investigated at different pH values with the Tris−HCl buffer (6.5–9.0). As demonstrated in Fig. S4A, the maximum ratiometric fluorescence sensing efficiency was obtained in pH 8.5 with brightest yellow color. As to the response time of the proposed CDs-AuNCs sensor, as can be seen in Fig. S4B, the fluorescence emission spectra of CDs-AuNCs displayed a significant change within 1 min as the addition of TC and then showed almost stably over further time. To ensure that the reaction reached equilibrium, the response time was optimized to 7 min. That is, pH 8.5 and 7 min as response time were are selected as the optimized conditions for TC determination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the ratiometric fluorescence sensing
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimal conditions, the sensitivity performance of the sensor was investigated by tracking the fluorescence intensities and the color variation versus TC concentration. As depicted in Fig. 4A, with increasing concentration of TC, the blue and red fluorescence peaks at 423 and 660 nm of CDs-AuNCs sensor were gradually quenched, while the green-emission peak at 520 nm based on the sensitization of TC was enhanced. The triple-emission intensity ratios of (I 520/I 423/I 660)/(I 520/I 423/I 660)0 of CDs-AuNCs exhibited a good linear correlation to TC concentrations in the range of 0.5–40 μM with a correlation coefficient of 0.9973 (Fig. 4B). Based on 3σ/s, where σ is the standard deviation of the blank measurements and s represents the slope of the calibration curve, the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.056 μM for TC. Moreover, based on the simultaneous increasing and decreasing of fluorescence intensity in TC recognition, the changes of the three emission peaks resulted in the significant and abundant color variation. Under a 365 nm UV light, the initial blue purple to pink, orange red, yellow, and finally yellow green can be easily recognized by the naked eye (the inset of Fig. 4). The rich fluorescence color variation of the triple-emission sensor provided an effective new idea for rapid and convenient TC identification.

In addition, compared with other single and dual emission fluorescence detection methods for TC [28–33] (Table S1), this detection strategy can realize the visual detection of TC with the naked eye more sensitively and clearly through the wider color variation covering the blue-red-green window. Although the LOD of our work is not the most precise, as a visualized fluorescence detection process, this method still provides high sensitivity and wide detection range, as well as simpler preparation without any complicated functionalization, which based on the low-toxicity fluorescent material.

In order to verify the selectivity of the prepared triple-emission sensor for the TC detection, several TC analogues were selected for examination. These included other tetracycline antibiotics, quinolone antibiotics, sulphonamide antibiotics, clindamycin, and several other potentially influential interferents such as amino acids and glutathione. As shown in Fig. 5, the triple-emission fluorescence ratio for the TC assay was much higher than the other analogues and showed an orange yellow color under UV light. Among the analogues, oxytetracycline (OTC) also had a relatively high fluorescence ratio, but was weaker than the effect of TC. The sensitization effect of chlortetracycline (CTC) was accompanied by an increase in fluorescence intensity at 423 nm, which resulted in a less pronounced change in the fluorescence ratio. The other classes of antibiotics and additional interferents will not be sensitized with OVA, so the fluorescence ratios didn’t change significantly and the colors were almost identical to that of the blank sample without reactants. The above experimental results illustrate that the prepared triple-emission sensor can specifically identify tetracycline antibiotics, which is a high selective sensor and has good application in practice.

To further validate the practical applicability of the developed method, the CDs-AuNCs sensor was applied to the detection of TC in real water samples, including river water and reservoir water. The results of the data obtained after samples measurement were listed in Table 1. The real samples spiked with TC at different concentrations (5, 10 and 25 μM) had satisfactory recoveries in the range of 90.3–106.0% and relative standard deviations (RSDs) in the range of 2.1–4.2%. A further conventional HPLC method was used to test the accuracy of this method and the values obtained were almost identical to the sensor assay. The ternary-emission sensor was proved to be accurate and reliable for detecting the TC in real environmental samples.

In summary, a triple-emission ratiometric fluorescence sensor (CDs-AuNCs) for the rapidly and visual determination of TC was successfully constructed based on the CDs hybridized OVA stabilized AuNCs nanocomposite. In the presence of a trace amount of TC, the fluorescence intensity at 520 nm of CDs-AuNCs enhanced effectively since the sensitization effect of OVA. Meanwhile, the fluorescence emission peaks centered at 423 and 660 nm were obviously quenched by TC through the IFE and dynamic quenching process. More prominently, the color evolution range covered the blue purple-pink-orange red-yellow-yellow green window can be easily observed by the naked eye. The CDs-AuNCs sensors benefited from the merits of the simple construction without any complicated functionalization, highly selectivity and sensitivity towards TC detection with excellent practicability and reliability, and the wide-range and profuse color variation naked-eye analysis of TC. The design and combination of the more abundant and diversified fluorescent materials will still remain the challenges for the preparation of future triple-emission fluorescence sensor. We envision that the presented triple-emission ratiometric fluorescence strategy can accelerate the development of multi-emission visual fluorescence sensing and can play the important role of rapid onsite visual detection in environmental monitoring, food inspection and safety assessment.

Jiaqi Miao: Conceptualization, Validation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Wan Ji: Data curation, Methodology, Investigation, Software. Jialuo Yu: Conceptualization, Supervision, Validation, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Jianxia Cheng: Data curation, Formal analysis. Yan Huang: Investigation, Project administration. Maryam Arabi: Methodology, Investigation. Na Zhou: Validation, Investigation. Bowei Li: Funding acquisition, Supervision. Zhiyang Zhang: Formal analysis, Supervision. Lingxin Chen: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Xiaoyan Wang: Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition, Validation, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21804010, 21575159, 41776110, 21976209, 22006162), the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2020QB105), the Science and Technology Innovation Development Plan of Yantai of China (2020YT06000898), the Chinese Academy of Sciences President’s International Fellowship Initiative (2019PC0050), Taishan Scholar Project Special Funding (Grant No. ts20190962, ts202103134) and Key Deployment Project of Centre for Ocean Mega-Research of Science, Chinese Academy of Sciences (COMQ2020Q11).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2023.133636.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Jiaqi Miao is currently a master’s candidate, joint-educated, at Binzhou Medical University, and Yantai Institute of Coastal Zone Research, Chinese Academy of Sciences, since 2021. Her current research interests are fluorescent and molecularly imprinted polymers based chemical sensors for environmental analysis.

Wan Ji is a postgraduate at School of Pharmacy, Binzhou Medical University and the Yantai Institute of Coastal Zone Research, Chinese Academy of Sciences. She is dedicated to the study of fluorescence nanosensors for drug molecular detection.

Jialuo Yu is currently as an engineer at Yantai Institute of Coastal Zone Research, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Her current research interests focus on the development of fluorescence based nanosensors and the applications in environmental analysis and bioimaging.

Jianxia Cheng is currently a master’s candidate, joint-educated, at Binzhou Medical University, and Yantai Institute of Coastal Zone Research, Chinese Academy of Sciences, since 2020. Her current research focus is on the detection and analysis of surface enhanced Raman spectroscopy.
Yan Huang is a professor at School of Pharmacy, Binzhou Medical University. His research interests focus on functional fluorescent probe, theranostics, and functional nanomaterial.

Maryam Arabi is currently working as a special research assistant at the Yantai Institute of Coastal Zone Research, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Her current research interests include green chemistry, synthesis, the application of molecularly imprinted polymers, and advanced functional materials for analytical and biomedical applications.

Na Zhou is currently as an engineer at Yantai Institute of Coastal Zone Research, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Her research interests focus on the basic research on detection of chemical and biological environmental chemical pollutants using HPLC and HPLC-mass spectrometry.

Bowei Li is an associate professor at Yantai Institute of Coastal Zone Research, Chinese Academy of Sciences. His research interests focus on the environmental monitoring, point-of-care testing, and other fields of basic research on chemical and biological using microfluidic chip platform.

Zhiyang Zhang is a professor at the Yantai Institute of Coastal Zone Research, Chinese Academy of Sciences. His research focuses on plasmonic nanomaterials and environmental applications. Currently, he is working on plasmonic sensors, Raman spectroscopy, microfluidics and pollutant analysis.

Lingxin Chen is a professor at Yantai Institute of Coastal Zone Research, Chinese Academy of Sciences. His current research interests are pollutant identification, analysis/monitoring technologies, and the development of nanoscale analytical methods based on novel materials such as molecularly imprinted polymers. As a corresponding author, he has published more than 400 peer-reviewed articles in journals such as Nature Sustainability and Nature Communications. He was selected as a Clarivate Highly Cited Researcher in the cross-field category in 2020, 2021 and 2022.

Xiaoyan Wang is a professor at School of Pharmacy, Binzhou Medical University. Her research interests focus on the studies of functionalized nanoparticles for developing nanoscale biochemical analysis methods and molecular imprinting-based sample pretreatment technology.
